Which cases correspond to underfitting or overfitting?
4
High Bias
= being consistently wrong.
High Variance
= being unpredictably inconsistent.

Bias–Variance and Model Behavior
Underfitting
Overfitting
5
Low Bias, Low Variance
Predictions are accurate and consistent.
Model is both correct on average and stable.
Low Bias, High Variance
Predictions are accurate on average but highly unstable.
Model is sensitive to small changes in data (overfitting risk).
High Bias, Low Variance
Predictions are consistently wrong in the same direction.
Model is too simple (underfitting).
High Bias, High Variance
Predictions are inaccurate and inconsistent.
Model is both wrong and unstable.

Why use cross-validation?
When the dataset is small
When a single train–test split is unreliable

11
k-Fold Cross-Validation
Split data into k equal folds
Train on k−1 folds
Validate on the remaining fold
Repeat k times
Average the validation results
Purpose:
reduce variance in performance estimation.

Understanding the Contours with L1 Regularization
19
The ellipses are contours of equal training loss.
Moving toward the center → lower loss
The center → minimum without regularization
L
1
Norm Ball
diamond
The diamond shape is the L1 constraint region.
It represents all parameter values with limited total absolute magnitude.
Where is the solution?
The first point where a loss contour touches the L1 boundary.
Why does L1 create sparsity?
The L1 ball has sharp corners on the axes.
Since contours often touch the diamond at a corner, one parameter becomes exactly zero.
That is why
L1 produces sparse solutions
.
L1 regularization pushes the solution toward the axes, making some weights exactly zero.

When Does L1 Create Sparsity?
Suppose we minimize:
24
Add L1 Constraint:
Suppose c = 2.
The contour hits a corner of the L1 diamond, so one coefficient becomes zero.
You get exact zeros when:
One coordinate is much smaller than the others.
The L1 ball boundary is reached at a corner.
The soft-thresholding step eliminates a variable.
Important Clarification
L1 encourages sparsity.
It does not guarantee sparsity in every example.
In higher dimensions, sparsity becomes much more common.
In 2D, it depends strongly on geometry.

Shrinkage vs. Sparsity
25
L1 Constraint (Diamond)
Has sharp corners.
Contour often becomes tangent at a corner.
One coefficient becomes exactly zero.
Produces sparse solutions.
L2 Constraint (Circle)
Has smooth boundary.
Contour becomes tangent on a curved edge.
Coefficients shrink smoothly.
Does not produce sparsity.

Effect: strongest sparsity control
Effect: sparse solutions

27
L1: Weights shrink by a constant amount toward 0.
L2: Weights shrink by an amount which is proportional to w.
For a weight that has a large magnitude, L1 regularization shrinks the weight much less than L2 regularization does.
By contrast, when |w| is small, L1 regularization shrinks the weight much more than L2 regularization.
Summary
Sparsity: L1 > L2
Stability: L2 > L1
Computational complexity: L2 > L1

What Is Dropout?
Dropout Rate = 50%
Dropout for this layer
Half of neurons removed each iteration.
28
Applied only during training
Dropout reduces overfitting by making the network robust to missing features.

Why Does Dropout Work?
Why Dropout Reduces Overfitting
Prevents neurons from relying on specific other neurons.
Forces each neuron to learn independently useful features
Acts like training many smaller subnetworks
Improves generalization
29

Dropout: Training vs Test
Inference with Dropout
All neurons are active
We scale activations (or weights) appropriately
Equivalent to averaging many subnetworks

Alleviate overfitting
